subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9! In this unit you will learn how to add and subtract fractions. Adding and subtracting fractions is really just a way of combining and separating parts of a whole. You already do this in your head more often than you realise. When Amina pours 1/4 cup of sugar into a bowl and then adds 1/2 cup more, she is adding fractions. When Yusuf uses 3/8 of a plank of wood for one shelf and 1/4 for another, and wants to know how much wood he has used in total, he is adding fractions too.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky, so you are never learning alone.

Why do fractions matter so much? Because the world is full of parts of things. A recipe rarely asks for a whole cup of everything. A trader at the Hargeisa market sells half a kilo of rice or three-quarters of a metre of cloth. A builder measures fractions of a plank. Time itself is measured in fractions of an hour. When you become confident adding and subtracting fractions, you gain a tool you will reach for again and again, and you build the foundation you need for multiplying fractions, working with decimals and percentages, and much of the algebra that comes later.

Keep a pencil and paper beside you as you read. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by looking at it – you must do it. Draw the fraction bars, write out every step, and say the rule aloud as you work. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit rests on one simple idea that we will return to again and again: you can only add or subtract parts that are the same size. Hold on to that idea and everything else will follow.
Add and subtract fractions that have the same denominator.
Explain the golden rule: fractions must have the same denominator before you can add or subtract them.
Find a common denominator for two fractions, especially when one denominator is a multiple of the other.
Change a fraction into an equivalent fraction with a chosen denominator.
Add and subtract fractions with different denominators.
Simplify a fraction to its lowest terms, and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
Use visual models – fraction walls, bars and number lines – to support and check your work.

concepts.title: The Golden Rule of Adding and Subtracting Fractions
concepts.explanation: Everything in this unit grows from one single idea, so let us plant it firmly right at the start. You can only add or subtract fractions when they have the same denominator. The denominator tells you the size of each piece. If the pieces are not the same size, you cannot simply count them together. Think of it like this. Imagine Farah has some fruit. You cannot add 2 apples and 3 oranges and get 5 apples – the fruits are different. But you *can* say she has 5 pieces of fruit, because now you have described them…
concepts.example: Everything in this unit grows from one single idea, so let us plant it firmly right at the start. You can only add or subtract fractions when they have the same denominator. The denominator tells you the size of each…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting with the Same Denominator
concepts.explanation: When fractions share a denominator, the work is wonderfully simple. Here is the rule: ⭐ Same-Denominator Rule To ADD: add the numerators (top numbers) and keep the denominator the same. To SUBTRACT: subtract the numerators and keep the denominator the same. Never add or subtract the denominators. The bottom number stays as it is.
concepts.example: Let us see why this makes sense. Suppose a pizza in the Kismaayo cafe is cut into 8 equal slices. Each slice is 1/8 of the pizza. If Hassan eats 3 slices, he has eaten 3/8. If his friend Omar eats 2 slices, he has eaten…

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions – The Key That Unlocks Everything
concepts.explanation: Before you can add fractions with different denominators, you need one powerful skill: making equivalent fractions. Two fractions are equivalent when they name exactly the same amount, even though they are written with different numbers. For example, 1/2, 2/4 and 4/8 are all the same size – half of the whole. You make an equivalent fraction by multiplying the top and the bottom by the same number. Whatever you do to the numerator, you must do to the denominator, because you are really just cutting each piece into…
concepts.example: A fraction wall helps you *see* this. Picture a whole bar, then the same-length bar cut into halves, then quarters, then eighths, all stacked one above the other:

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting with Different Denominators
concepts.explanation: This is the heart of the unit, and now you have all the tools you need. When two fractions have different denominators, you must first make the denominators the same. Here is the four-step method you will use again and again: Find a common denominator – a number that both denominators divide into.
concepts.example: Convert both fractions into equivalent fractions with that common denominator.

concepts.title: Improper Fractions and Mixed Numbers
concepts.explanation: Sometimes when you add fractions, the numerator ends up bigger than the denominator. A fraction like this – for example 6/4 – is called an improper fraction, and it means you have more than one whole. It is often neater to write such an answer as a mixed number, which is a whole number joined with a fraction. Here is how. Add 3/4 + 3/4. Same denominator, so add the numerators: 3 + 3 = 6, keep the four: 6/4. Now 6/4 means six quarters. Four quarters make one whole, and two quarters are left over, so 6/4 = 1 2/4.…
concepts.example: To turn an improper fraction into a mixed number, ask how many times the denominator fits into the numerator, and what is left over. For 9/8: eight fits into nine once with 1 left over, so 9/8 = 1 1/8. For 7/3: three…

concepts.title: Picturing Fractions – Diagrams and Number Lines
concepts.explanation: Good mathematicians do not only calculate; they also picture what they are doing, because a picture catches mistakes that numbers alone can hide. There are two pictures worth mastering for this unit. Diagrams (bars and circles). To show 1/2 + 1/4, draw a rectangle and divide it into 4 equal parts, because quarters are the smallest pieces here. Shade 2 parts for the half (since 1/2 = 2/4), then shade 1 more part for the quarter. You have now shaded 3 of the 4 parts, so the total is 3/4. The picture and the…
concepts.example: Number lines. Mark a line from 0 to 1 with steps of a quarter: 0, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1. To add 1/2 + 1/4, start at 1/2 and jump one quarter to the right, landing on 3/4. To subtract, jump to the left instead. Number lines…

methods.title: How to add or subtract fractions with different denominators
steps: Find a common denominator (often the larger denominator, when one is a multiple of the other).
Change each fraction into an equivalent fraction with that denominator.
Add or subtract the numerators; keep the common denominator.
Simplify the answer, and change any improper fraction to a mixed number.

methods.title: How to change an improper fraction to a mixed number
steps: Ask how many times the denominator fits into the numerator – that is the whole-number part.
The remainder becomes the new numerator, over the same denominator.
Simplify the fraction part if you can. Example: 6/4 = 1 2/4 = 1 1/2.

methods.title: How to subtract a fraction from a whole
steps: Rewrite the whole as a fraction with the denominator you need, for example 1 = 8/8.
Subtract the numerators; keep the denominator.
Simplify. Example: 1 − 3/8 = 8/8 − 3/8 = 5/8.

methods.title: Different denominators (one is a multiple of the other)
steps: Solution: Four is a multiple of two, so use 4 as the common denominator.
Change 1/2 into quarters: 1/2 = 2/4.
The 1/4 stays.

methods.title: Different denominators, subtraction
steps: Solution: Eight is a multiple of four, so use 8.
Change 3/4 into eighths: 3/4 = 6/8.
The 1/8 stays.

methods.title: Different denominators that need multiplying
steps: Solution: Six is a multiple of three, so use 6.
Change 2/3 into sixths: 2/3 = (2×2)/(3×2) = 4/6.
The 1/6 stays.

workedExamples.title: Same denominator, addition
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators are the same, so add the numerators: 3 + 2 = 5, and keep the 8. The answer is 5/8. Check for simplifying: 5 and 8 share no factor, so 5/8 is already in simplest form.

workedExamples.title: Same denominator, subtraction with simplifying
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the numerators: 7 − 3 = 4, keep the 10, giving 4/10. Now simplify – both 4 and 10 divide by 2 – so 4/10 = 2/5.

workedExamples.title: A missing numerator
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators are all 9, so we only work with the tops. What adds to 4 to make 7? Since 4 + 3 = 7, the missing numerator is 3. So 4/9 + 3/9 = 7/9.

workedExamples.title: Different denominators (one is a multiple of the other)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Four is a multiple of two, so use 4 as the common denominator. Change 1/2 into quarters: 1/2 = 2/4. The 1/4 stays. Add: 2/4 + 1/4 = 3/4. This cannot be simplified, so 3/4 is the answer.

workedExamples.title: Different denominators, subtraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Eight is a multiple of four, so use 8. Change 3/4 into eighths: 3/4 = 6/8. The 1/8 stays. Subtract: 6/8 − 1/8 = 5/8. It is already simplest.

workedExamples.title: Different denominators that need multiplying
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Six is a multiple of three, so use 6. Change 2/3 into sixths: 2/3 = (2×2)/(3×2) = 4/6. The 1/6 stays. Add: 4/6 + 1/6 = 5/6. Since 5 and 6 share no factor, 5/6 is the final answer.

workedExamples.title: An answer that becomes a mixed number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Same denominator, so add the numerators: 3 + 3 = 6, keep the 4, giving 6/4. This is an improper fraction. Four quarters make one whole with two quarters left, so 6/4 = 1 2/4, and 2/4 simplifies to 1/2. The tidy answer is 1 1/2.

workedExamples.title: Subtracting from a whole
workedExamples.prompt: A whole board is used up by 3/8. How much is left?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write the whole as eighths: 1 = 8/8. Then 8/8 − 3/8 = 5/8 of the board is left. Notice that 3/8 + 5/8 = 8/8 = 1, which confirms the answer.

workedExamples.title: Calculate and simplify
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate and simplify: a) 2/5 + 1/10 b) 3/8 + 1/4 c) 4/5 − 1/10

workedExamples.title: Calculate, giving your answer as a mixed number
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate, giving your answer as a mixed number: a) 3/4 + 3/4 b) 5/6 + 5/6
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 3: challenge (mixed numbers and multi-step) guidance and explain each step.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing fraction
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing fraction: a) 1/2 + ___ = 3/4 b) 5/8 − ___ = 1/4 c) ___ + 1/6 = 1/2
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 3: challenge (mixed numbers and multi-step) guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Fraction Strip Addition
activities.materials: paper strips of equal length, a ruler, coloured pencils, scissors.
steps: Cut strips into fraction pieces: one whole, two halves, four quarters, and eight eighths.
Lay pieces side by side to show 1/4 + 1/4 = 2/4 = 1/2.
Now show 1/2 + 1/4 by first swapping the half for two quarters, then combining.
Challenge: build 3/8 + 1/4 and read the total off your strips.
Discovery: What do you notice about adding fractions once all the pieces are the same size?

activities.title: The Fraction Restaurant
activities.materials: paper circles for pizzas, coloured pencils.
steps: You own a small cafe in Kismaayo. Draw circular pizzas and cut them into equal slices.
Customer 1 orders 1/3 of a pizza plus 1/6 more. Draw it and find the total.
Customer 2 orders 3/4 of a pizza but returns 1/4. Draw it and find what they keep.
Customer 3 orders 1/2 plus 1/8. Draw it and find the total.
Business challenge: If you have 2 whole pizzas, can you fill all three orders? Show your working.

activities.title: Recipe Fraction Math
activities.materials: a recipe card (real or invented), measuring cups if you have them.
steps: Write a simple recipe using fractions, for example 1/2 cup flour, 1/4 cup sugar, 1/3 cup milk.
You have already added 1/4 cup of flour from the 1/2 cup needed. How much flour is still to add?
Combine two batches: how much flour do two lots of 1/2 cup make altogether?
Family task: cook or measure together and check the fractions in real cups.

activities.title: Fraction Number-Line Walk
activities.materials: a long paper strip and a marker.
steps: Draw a number line from 0 to 2, marked in eighths.
Start at 1/8 and walk 3/8 to the right. Where do you land? (Write it as a simplified fraction.)
Start at 1 1/4 and walk back 1/2. Where are you now?
Make up two walking problems of your own and give them to a friend or family member.

activities.title: Fraction Art Mosaic
activities.materials: grid paper (or a hand-drawn grid) and coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a grid of equal squares – sixteen squares works well.
Colour 1/4 of the squares red and 1/8 of them blue.
Colour 3/8 of the squares green. Add red, blue and green together – what fraction is coloured so far?
The rest are yellow. What fraction is yellow? (Hint: subtract the coloured fraction from the whole.)

activities.title: Design a Fraction Board Game (Family or Group Task)
activities.materials: paper for a board, a coin or dice, small counters.
steps: Draw a winding track of squares from Start to Finish.
Write a fraction addition or subtraction problem on each square, such as 1/6 + 2/6 or 3/4 − 1/4.
Players move only when they solve their square correctly; the first to Finish wins.
Investigation: after playing, sort your problems into same-denominator and different-denominator piles. Which felt harder, and why?

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: When do two fractions need the same denominator before you can add them? How do you find a common denominator? Which real-life situations in your day use adding or subtracting fractions? Which activity helped you understand equivalent fractions best, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: The Golden Rule
rules.text: You can only add or subtract fractions when they have the SAME denominator. Same denominator? Add or subtract the numerators; keep the denominator. Different denominators? Make them the same first, then add or subtract.

rules.title: Equivalent Fraction Rule
rules.text: Multiply the top and the bottom by the same number to make an equivalent fraction. 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8. The amount stays the same.

rules.title: Simplifying Rule
rules.text: Divide the top and the bottom by the same number to reach lowest terms. 6/8 = 3/4; 4/6 = 2/3; 10/15 = 2/3.

terms: The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Different fractions naming the same amount (1/2 = 2/4)
Divide top and bottom by the same number to reach lowest terms
A whole number joined to a fraction (1 3/4)
commonMistakes: Make the denominators match before adding
Divide top and bottom by 2 to get 3/4
Change it to the mixed number 1 1/2

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: What does “Numerator” mean?
options: The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Different fractions naming the same amount (1/2 = 2/4)
questions.answer: The top number – how many parts you have
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator means The top number – how many parts you have.

questions.question: What does “Denominator” mean?
options: The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Different fractions naming the same amount (1/2 = 2/4)
Divide top and bottom by the same number to reach lowest terms
questions.answer: The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Math Words & Symbols reference.

questions.question: What does “Equivalent fractions” mean?

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky, so you are never learning alone.

I can why do fractions matter so much? Because the world is full of parts of things. A recipe rarely asks for a whole cup of everything. A trader at the Hargeisa market sells half a kilo of rice or three-quarters of a metre of cloth. A builder measures fractions of a plank. Time itself is measured in fractions of an hour. When you become confident adding and subtracting fractions, you gain a tool you will reach for again and again, and you build the foundation you need for multiplying fractions, working with decimals and percentages, and much of the algebra that comes later.

I can keep a pencil and paper beside you as you read. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by looking at it – you must do it. Draw the fraction bars, write out every step, and say the rule aloud as you work. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit rests on one simple idea that we will return to again and again: you can only add or subtract parts that are the same size. Hold on to that idea and everything else will follow.
I can add and subtract fractions that have the same denominator.
I can explain the golden rule: fractions must have the same denominator before you can add or subtract them.
I can find a common denominator for two fractions, especially when one denominator is a multiple of the other.
I can change a fraction into an equivalent fraction with a chosen denominator.
I can add and subtract fractions with different denominators.
